data.title: Fiverr: The Bleeding Will Be Tough To Stop As Growth Slows (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The most prominent theme to shake the markets in 2026 is the notion of a "SaaSpocalypse," or the idea that vibe coding and agentic AI will soon unseat established software companies and break the recurring-revenue business models that Wall Street and Silicon Valley have come to rely on. While the recent Q4 earnings season showcases that most software companies are embracing the AI revolution from a position of strength, there is certainly one example of a company that is being exposed as a net loser from AI, and that's Fiverr (FVRR).

Fiverr, which alongside Upwork (UPWK) is among the largest marketplaces for freelance and contract workers, has seen a sharp contraction in growth rates, and the company's recently released guidance for 2026 doesn't call for this year to be the light at the end of the tunnel. Shares of Fiverr recently fell ~6% after reporting very weak Q4 results, extending a massive slide over the past year that has wiped out two-thirds of the company's value.

I last wrote a buy rating on Fiverr in November, when the stock was trading at $22 per share. I'll cut to the chase: my bullishness on the company, which was mainly based on a cheap valuation, was misplaced. Over the past few months, two important factors have changed. First, Fiverr is no longer growing, and in fact, for FY26 the company expects revenue and adjusted EBITDA to decline: a clear signal that the company isn't just facing macro struggles, but potentially a loss of relevance as agentic AI can take over many of the functions and use cases that Fiverr used to fill. Second, there are now plenty of cheap bargains on the stock market thanks to the so-called SaaSpocalypse. High-quality names, such as Atlassian (TEAM) and GitLab (GTLB), have gone from momentum stocks to bargain-basement value plays in the span of just a few short months. With that in mind, the main appeal of Fiverr, which is value, is far less unique and convincing of a bull case driver. Alongside deteriorating results, I'm dropping my rating on Fiverr by two notches to sell. I don't think any of the company's fundamental issues have any near-term resolution, and the stock will continue to be stuck in a rut until it can solve its growth challenges (if it ever does).

Let's address the elephant in the room that took Fiverr's stock down post-earnings: its terrible outlook for FY26, which is shown below:

The company is expecting revenue to contract between -3% y/y and -12% y/y. It's difficult for the company to claim that AI-related work is driving tailwinds for the company when, at the same time, it's expecting revenue to compress, and when GMV and active buyers are also showcasing extreme pressure.

Additionally disappointing is the company's adjusted EBITDA outlook, which calls for a y/y decline of -34% y/y to -13% y/y. The $70 million midpoint of that range reflects a 17.5% margin, which is 380bps below 21.3% margin in FY25.

Recall that Fiverr in Q3 promised a companywide restructuring that was intended to fold AI into the company's operations and boost efficiency. I had applauded the moves at the time, taking the restructuring as a signal that Fiverr was willing to streamline and shrink costs in response to slowing growth. This doesn't appear to be happening at all. In Q4, Fiverr announced a leadership transition that involved the decoupling of its President/CFO role. Details are showcased in the snapshot below:

To me, this looks simply like Fiverr is stacking up its C-suite. It's effectively reducing the duties and portfolio of Ofer Katz, the current President/CFO, while promoting two other executives to the C-suite in CFO and Chief Business Officer titles. While we certainly don't disagree that strong leadership is necessary to pull off a turnaround, it doesn't exactly send a strong signal that Fiverr is interested in downsizing when it's increasing its executive ranks and forecasting both revenue declines and margin deleverage for FY26.

All in all, the risk-reward equation has heavily tilted out of Fiverr's favor. I'm content to take a loss on this position and move on to investing in other names, especially with deep bargains now available in the software sector (where, relative to Fiverr, there is far less evidence of AI being an existential risk).

Q4 download

Let's now go through Fiverr's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Fiverr's revenue grew just 3% y/y to $107.2 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $109.0 million (+5% y/y) by a two-point margin. Revenue growth also decelerated five points versus 8% y/y growth in Q3. And we don't expect trends to get better anytime soon: Fiverr's guidance for Q1 calls for a midpoint decline of -3% y/y, with trends to worsen throughout FY26 (as -3% is the high end of the company's full year FY26 guidance).

Fiverr continues to insist that the future of the workplace and AI is what it calls "human in the loop", or having a human driver to orchestrate work that is performed by AI agents. Whether or not this is true, it doesn't appear that the company is showcasing its credibility to provide that human AI manager. Fiverr's active buyer pool shrank to 3.14 million, down -14% y/y and a loss of 162k net buyers relative to 3.30 million in Q3. This is evidence of the fact that as customers evaluate their workflows and try to integrate AI into their operations, the first line to be cut is contractors and consultants who are non-core to existing workstreams, directly impacting Fiverr's core use cases.

Previously, Fiverr had touted that revenue from AI-related development work was growing at a rapid clip - one of the reasons that I had been more positive on the stock. As recently as the Q2 shareholder letter, the company noted that demand for Fiverr freelancers for servicing implementation of vibe coding tools like Lovable and Bolt was growing at a 5-10x y/y pace.

While the company still touts AI-native work as a core reason for growth in spend per active buyer, it's no longer disclosing rapid y/y growth rates, which to me suggests that sequential growth may not be as impressive. We always knew that AI would drive additional business use cases as well as losses in core functions that are now easily automatable via agents. It's almost certain that vibe coding/AI expertise projects are still driving new business for Fiverr, even if not growing at a 5-10x pace. But now I view AI as more of a net-loss for the company, as the company is also losing a lot more lower-level projects that can now be handled by AI (evidence for this is clear in the company's sharp y/y revenue deceleration).

Management continues to argue that it doesn't regret the loss of lower-value, lower-spending buyers and lower-dollar transactional work. The company is asking the markets for patience, as it expects the transition to higher-value, AI-driven work to cure the company's revenue problems in 4-6 quarters. Per CEO Micha Kaufman's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

There will be displacement in lower-value transactional work. We are already seeing that dynamic. At the same time, demand for higher-value specialized work is accelerating at a healthy double-digit rate. As work becomes more nuanced and complex, matching talent becomes harder, not easier [...]

A few months ago, we initiated a company-wide restructuring to accelerate this shift. We have since developed a multiyear execution plan built around 4 pillars: the first is matching, building advanced semantic and reasoning layer powered by proprietary data to enable AI-native talent matching. The second is product, transforming the experience across matching, fulfillment, collaboration and talent management. The third is go-to-market, expanding into enterprise and AI-native distribution channels with scalable growth engines. The fourth is operational excellence, becoming an AI-native organization across engineering, product and operations.

We expect tangible impact within 4 to 6 quarters, including a stronger high-value work flywheel and proven AI native growth loops. These milestones will position us for meaningful revenue expansion in the years ahead. Let me be clear, this is the moment to lean in. AI is not shrinking the market for human talent. It is reshaping access and expanding ambition."

Needless to say, it's hard to put stock in these claims when all current metrics and even the company's current outlook point to shrinkage.

On the profitability front, Fiverr's adjusted EBITDA grew 23% y/y to $91.6 million, with adjusted EBITDA margins rising 230bps y/y to 21.3%.

With top-line shrinkage expected next year, however, the company isn't expecting these margin tailwinds to be sustained.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $12, Fiverr trades at a market cap of $456.1 million. Netting off the $282.9 million of cash (against zero debt) on Fiverr's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of just $173.2 million.

Against the $70 million midpoint of Fiverr's latest adjusted EBITDA outlook range, Fiverr trades at just 2.5x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. Needless to say, that's a deep bargain multiple, but it reflects a business that is threading an existential crisis right now, with AI expected to severely cut into demand for its freelancers.

We're no longer willing to bet on this stock merely for being cheap, especially since many cheap software stocks are on the chopping block at the moment that have visible proof of stronger revenue retention trends in their Q4 earnings prints. Sell Fiverr and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Baron Focused Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Baron Focused Growth Fund Shareholder,

Performance

Baron Focused Growth Fund® (the Fund) finished the last quarter of 2025 strongly, with a gain of 12.34% (Institutional Shares) compared with a 0.33% increase for the Russell 2500 Growth Index (the Benchmark). This brought the Fund's 2025 gain to 22.26% versus a 10.31% increase for the Benchmark. While the quarterly outperformance was led by two new capital raises for Space Exploration Technologies Corp. and X.AI Holdings Corp. at valuations significantly higher than previous raises, the Fund also saw strength from its consumer-oriented investments including FIGS, Inc. (FIGS) , Hyatt Hotels Corporation (H) , and On Holding AG (ONON) , as the consumer remains quite resilient. This is despite continued concerns about higher inflation, interest rates, and a tighter labor market.

While we are pleased with the 2025 performance, we still see opportunity throughout the portfolio. Our companies continue to do quite well and are generating strong revenue growth while increasing investments to accelerate growth further. Most of our companies continue to maintain financial leverage below targeted levels, giving them enhanced flexibility to make investments, strategic acquisitions should something come available, or return capital to shareholders through buybacks should there be dislocations in stock prices.

Despite recent gains, a significant portion of the stocks in the portfolio continue to trade at multiples below historical levels. We believe this combination of strong revenue growth with well-positioned balance sheets and attractive valuations offers multiple avenues for potential returns for investors. As a result, we continue to view the portfolio as compelling, with a favorable risk/reward profile and are putting new cash to work in both existing and new names.

Further, we believe there is still a ton of capital remaining on the sidelines waiting to be invested, including private equity firms who continue to raise new funds. We believe as rates continue to move lower over the next year, public to private transactions and strategic acquisitions should accelerate, which should further support valuations and our investments.

We continue to believe these businesses have strong competitive advantages with underpenetrated growth opportunities ahead of them and robust balance sheets to finance their growth. We believe valuations are attractive at current levels and continue to see accelerated levels of insider buying and company share repurchase activity, a key pillar that gives us increased confidence in our investment theses for these companies and expected stock returns over time.

In the near term, we continue to believe that inflation will remain at or below the historic 3% to 4% annualized level, and interest rates will approximate the rate of inflation. This has been the case since World War II. We believe that is a favorable environment for businesses that are growing significantly faster than the rate of inflation and the 7% nominal annualized growth rate of our economy.

The Fund has continued to outperform its Benchmark over the prior 3-, 5-, and 10- year periods, generating significant excess returns with much less than market risk. Over the past 3-, 5-, and 10- year periods, the Fund has generated 1,227, 870, and 1,042 bps of outperformance, respectively, with volatility measured as the Beta of the Fund that is 30%, 15% and 5%, respectively, less than the market. As a result, the Fund's Sharpe ratio, a measure of risk-adjusted return, was significantly higher than the Benchmark for each of these periods.

We believe these strong returns with downside management of risk are due to our research-based investment process. Our research enables us to identify and understand businesses' competitive advantages, differentiation, long-term growth prospects, and exceptional people; and it allows us to invest in these businesses for the long term at what we believe are attractive valuations relative to what these businesses can become. As a result, as shown in the table below, the Fund has outperformed its Benchmark for all respective periods including since its inception on May 31, 1996. Since its inception as a private partnership almost 30 years ago, the Fund has increased 14.16% annually. This compares to an 8.31% annualized return for the Benchmark and a 10.03% annualized return for the Russell 3000 Index that measures the performance of the largest 3,000 U.S. companies.

Aside from two of our private investments, SpaceX and xAI successfully completing capital raises at valuations significantly higher than previous marks, performance in the fourth quarter was led by our exposure to consumer-focused investments as the consumer remains resilient despite macro worries.

These gains were partially offset by continued concerns about the introduction of AI into the economy and those businesses that could be impacted most from the new competition. These included our subscription-based software and platform investments such as Spotify Technology S.A., CoStar Group, Inc., and Guidewire Software, Inc. However, while the increased competition hurt the valuation of these stocks in the quarter, it has not impacted financials, and these companies continue to generate strong revenue growth and margins in line with company and investor expectations.

Shares of FIGS increased 69.8% in the fourth quarter and added 161 bps to performance. The company continues to generate strong results, and revenue has recently accelerated from investments made over the past year. This is despite making a conscious decision to pull back on promotions. The company is now seeing more normal purchasing and replenishment trends while also benefitting from specific initiatives around product and marketing that are resonating with its customers. FIGS is also seeing better efficiencies out of its new fulfillment center that are resulting in strong margin improvement as well. We continue to believe FIGS can take share in the attractive $80 billion global health care apparel industry. Health care remains one of the fastest growing employment industries in the U.S. and FIGS benefits from being a need-based replenishment item. The company is well positioned to take share in the industry through its premium, high-quality products. In addition, we believe FIGS will also see growth from its initiatives to grow stores, business to business revenue, and expand internationally. We believe the revenue acceleration combined with margin expansion and strong cash generation should leave the company well positioned for further growth over time.

Shares of Hyatt Hotels increased 13.1% and added 60 bps to performance in the fourth quarter as the company reported strong RevPAR and unit growth rates despite concerns about a deterioration in the macro economy. In addition, the company also came to an agreement with Chase to extend its credit card agreement with stronger economics for Hyatt given the increase in the company's World of Hyatt membership. Finally, the company continues to sell its owned hotels at accretive rates and is using the proceeds to buy back the company's stock. Hyatt continues to have an investment grade balance sheet with 90% of the business coming through fees, yet trades at a discount to peers despite similar growth and mix of business. We believe this discount should narrow over time as investors see the continued growth and resilience of its business model.

Premium footwear and apparel brand On Holding increased 9.8% and added 55 bps to performance in the fourth quarter. The company continues to generate strong revenue growth with accelerated margin expansion despite concerns about tariffs and increased competition from Nike. On's strong brand and premium positioning is allowing it to offset tariff exposure through selective price increases, while demand for its products remains resilient. The company should continue to grow for many years to come while taking share in the highly attractive global sportswear market. They remain a small player in a large growing market with just 2% of the global sports footwear market. We believe On has differentiated itself through its engineered solution and that the company's innovation capabilities should fuel share gains for many years. This growth should be supported by expansions across categories, retail outlets, and geographies. We believe the company should be able to grow revenue at a CAGR of over 20% the next few years leading to EBITDA growth of over 30%, which when combined with a mid-single-digit yield on free cash flow should set the stock up for strong returns in the years to come.

Spotify declined by 16.8% in the fourth quarter and detracted 130 bps from performance as investors were concerned with the decision by CEO Dan Ek to step down as CEO. In addition, further concerns about the timing of price increases and resulting margin expansion also frustrated investors. However, the company continues to institute price increases across multiple regions and complete negotiations with major record labels. User growth remains strong growing at a double-digit rate with high engagement and low churn even with price increases. The company remains on a path to increase gross margins through its high-margin artist promotions marketplace, growing podcast contribution, and ongoing investments in advertising where revenue growth is expected to accelerate this year. We continue to view Spotify as a long-term winner in music streaming with potential to reach 1 billion-plus subscribers by 2030.

CoStar declined by 20.3% in the fourth quarter and detracted 93 bps from performance as investors were concerned with a deceleration of growth in its core commercial real estate business and a slower-than-expected ramp of its residential business. However, we believe daily active users on its Homes.com platform should accelerate this year as its marketing investments begin to generate returns. Monthly active users have already reached 110 million and compare to Zillow's 250 million users. This is positive as CoStar is demonstrating that it can drive meaningful traffic growth to its platform. We believe the acceleration in investment over the past two years should drive organic growth on its Homes.com platform and expand the company's addressable market. We believe investors are currently attributing negative equity value to this. Over the next five years, we believe CoStar's residential investment could add at least $1 billion to annualized revenue at a significantly accretive margin. This would result in a 33% increase in today's $3 billion in revenue and an approximate 50% increase in EBITDA. Longer term, we believe this investment opportunity is several multiples of $1 billion of revenue. CoStar continues to hire new people for its commercial real estate sales business and should begin to see a rebound in net new bookings this year with continued strength in its retention rates, despite implementing price increases across its suite of products. It continues to have a strong balance sheet, with $2 billion of cash and just $1 billion of debt. We are not concerned with its residential investment and believe it should generate strong returns over time.

Property and casualty (P&C) insurance software vendor Guidewire declined 12.6% in the fourth quarter and detracted 62 bps from performance. However, the company continues to do quite well and after a multi-year transition period, the company's cloud transition is substantially complete and insurers are upgrading to the cloud at an accelerated rate. We believe that cloud will be the sole path forward, with annual recurring revenue ('ARR') benefiting from new customer wins and migrations of the existing customer base to the company's Insurance Suite Cloud. We also expect the company to shift R&D resources to product development from infrastructure investment, which should help drive cross-sales into its sticky installed base and potentially accelerate ARR over time. We are encouraged by Guidewire's subscription gross margin expansion, which improved by approximately 280 bps in its most

recently reported quarter. We believe Guidewire will be the critical software vendor for the global P&C insurance industry, capturing 30% to 50% of its $15 billion to $30 billion total addressable market and generating margins above 40%.

Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX) is a high-profile private company founded by Elon Musk. The company's primary focus is on developing and launching advanced rockets, satellites, and spacecrafts, with the ambitious long-term goal of making life multi-planetary. SpaceX is generating significant value with the rapid expansion of its Starlink broadband service. The company is successfully deploying a vast constellation of Starlink satellites in Earth's orbit, reporting substantial growth in active users, and regularly deploying new and more efficient hardware technology. Furthermore, SpaceX has established itself as a leading launch provider by offering highly reliable and cost-effective launches, leveraging the company's reusable launch technology. SpaceX capabilities extend to strategic services such as human spaceflight missions. Moreover, SpaceX is making tremendous progress on its newest rocket, Starship, which is the largest, most powerful rocket ever flown. This next-generation vehicle represents a significant leap forward in reusability and space exploration capabilities. We value SpaceX using prices of recent financing transactions.

X.AI Holdings Corp. was formed in early 2025 through the merger of X (formerly Twitter) and xAI, an AI company founded by Elon Musk in March 2023 with the mission to "understand the true nature of the universe." This strategic union paired xAI's large language models with X's real-time data and worldwide distribution, speeding Grok's development while providing X with transformative AI tools for search, personalization, and user engagement. Shortly after its founding, xAI released its AI model, Grok, which swiftly emerged as a top-tier contender. Fueling Grok's performance was the rapid deployment of xAI's data centers: Colossus 1 became operational in just 122 days with 100,000 GPUs, while Colossus 2's first 100,000 GPUs deployed even faster, positioning xAI to pioneer a 1-gigawatt training facility. The upcoming 5th version of Grok will use Colossus 2's expanded resources and is expected to mark further improvement in the model's capabilities. Such early results demonstrate xAI's innovation prowess and its prospects for enduring leadership in the highly competitive AI field. We value the stock based on material transaction in shares, leading to stock appreciation.

FIGS, Inc. designs and sells scrubwear for health care professionals through a digitally native, direct-to-consumer strategy. Shares rose after the company reported quarterly results that beat expectations and raised its outlook for revenue and profits for the remainder of the year. FIGS' revenue grew 8% due to robust customer demand for its health care apparel, supported by improving execution and normalizing industry trends. Demand for health care apparel remains largely non-discretionary and replenishment driven. The company also delivered stronger-than-expected profitability, benefiting from meaningful operating leverage while continuing to invest in the business. In addition, FIGS continues to expand its three key growth initiatives: international markets, TEAMS (its enterprise and group ordering business), and retail. Internationally, the company refined its growth strategy and is targeting 60 planned markets by year-end, up from 33. Within TEAMS, FIGS continues to add talent and develop new technology solutions to support growth. In retail, the company plans to open three stores (New York, Houston, and Chicago) with further expansion anticipated into 2026.

Top detractors from performance for the quarter

Guidewire Software, Inc. 2013 2.7 17.1 (12.55) (0.62)
Interactive Brokers Group, Inc. 2023 33.8 109.3 (6.42) (0.41)

Spotify Technology S.A. is a leading global digital music service, offering on-demand audio streaming through paid premium subscriptions and an ad-supported model. Shares of Spotify fell as richly valued stocks across a similar peer basket broadly underperformed. In our view, the company's fundamentals remain intact. Despite recent price hikes, user growth has continued at a double-digit year-over-year pace, with engagement remaining high. Spotify has proven to be a sticky subscription product with relative resilience in times of consumer uncertainty. The company has been on a path to structurally increase gross margins on an annual basis, aided by its high-margin artist promotions marketplace, growing contribution from podcasts, and ongoing investments in advertising. Spotify also continues to innovate across its platform, improving advertising, expanding into video, developing a Super Premium tier, and taking more market share. We still view Spotify as a long-term winner in music streaming with potential to reach 1 billion-plus monthly active users.

CoStar Group, Inc. is the leading provider of information and marketing services to the commercial and residential real estate industries. Shares fell as the company's net new sales came in below expectations. The stock has been weighed down by significant growth investment in CoStar's residential product, where sales performance has remained modest. That said, we are encouraged by improving momentum as the company builds out its dedicated residential sales force, enhances its customer targeting, and potentially benefits from changes in Multiple Listing Service practices. We also expect growth in CoStar's non-residential business to accelerate as sales productivity ramps and the sales team refocuses on core offerings, a trend likely to be amplified by 20% sales force growth in 2025 alone. We believe the value of CoStar's core non-residential business exceeds the current share price of the stock, suggesting that investors are ascribing little value to the long-term residential opportunity.

Duolingo, Inc. is the world's leading language-learning app, with over 135 million monthly active users. The company is known for its effective gamification strategy and high engagement. Shares declined during the quarter after the company guided to lower-than-expected bookings as it prioritized user experience. Although bookings and user growth appear unlikely to decelerate meaningfully from current levels, uncertainty around engagement metrics and the pace of monetization led us to exit the position and reallocate capital to other investment opportunities.

Investment Strategy and Portfolio Structure

We remain steadfast in our commitment to long-term investing in competitively advantaged, growth businesses. We continue to run a balanced portfolio of uncorrelated businesses to help reduce portfolio risk while generating strong excess returns over time. We believe this portfolio strategy is an effective way to mitigate risk and increase the purchasing power of your savings. While there will always be market volatility, we believe we can reduce that volatility via this portfolio that is approximately 80% as volatile as the market. This is due to the balanced nature of the portfolio as seen below with approximately 40% invested in high-growth disruptive investments that can generate revenue growth of as much as 20% to 30%; 20% of the portfolio in real irreplaceable assets that trade at significant discounts to replacement cost and where they would sell to private equity or another strategic buyer; and 15% in financial data businesses that have recurring revenue and earnings given the embedded nature of their products in the workflow of their customers; and the balance in core double-digit revenue growing businesses that are more mature in their lifecycle and generate earnings growth while using excess cash for dividend increases, share buybacks, and additional investments in the business in order to accelerate growth further.

As of December 31, 2025, the Fund owned 28 investments. From a quality standpoint, the Fund's investments have generally stronger long-term sales growth; higher EBITDA, operating, and free-cash-flow margins; and stronger returns on invested capital than the Benchmark with less financial leverage. We believe these metrics help limit risk in this focused portfolio and are why the portfolio has generated such strong risk-adjusted returns over time.

While focused, the Fund is diversified by sector. The Fund's weightings are significantly different than those of the Benchmark. For example, the Fund is heavily weighted to Consumer Discretionary businesses with 36.0% of net assets in this sector versus 11.7% for the Benchmark. The Fund has no exposure to Energy, Materials, or Utilities. We believe companies in these sectors can be cyclical, linked to commodity prices, and/or have little if any competitive advantage. data.title: Voya Solution Portfolios (Target Date) Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Voya Solution Portfolio Series is designed to specifically balance the evolving risk-return profiles of participants as they age to maximize the probability of a successful retirement.

Key takeaways

  • Global markets ended the year strong despite volatility from the U.S. government shutdown, softening job growth, and elevated valuation concerns. Foreign equities outperformed, while fixed income delivered positive but muted returns.
  • Equity fundamental factors remain supported by steady economic growth, easing inflation, and ongoing investment in productivity-enhancing technologies, while solid corporate balance sheets and gradually improving financial conditions create a constructive backdrop for earnings and risk assets.
  • The Portfolios posted positive absolute and positive relative returns for the period, outperforming their strategic allocation benchmarks on a gross- and net-of-fees basis.

Market review

Global markets ended the year strongly despite disruptions. The U.S. government shutdown weighed on sentiment, adding volatility amid slowing job growth and concerns over elevated artificial intelligence ('AI') valuations. Supportive earnings and central bank accommodation sustained risk appetite. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued easing, cutting rates 25 basis points in October and December, while expectations for further cuts fluctuated.

U.S. equities posted modest gains, though leadership shifted. Large cap value outperformed growth, reflecting rotation away from concentrated technology exposures, while defensive sectors such as health care led amid valuation concerns. Small cap stocks also rose but lagged large caps.

International equities outperformed on stronger earnings momentum and more attractive valuations. Japan benefited from renewed political clarity under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, reinforcing reform expectations. Emerging markets ('EM') were mixed; some regions gained from global growth and AI investment, while China lagged due to ongoing property weakness and deflation.

Fixed income delivered positive returns but trailed equities. Core bonds benefited from rate cuts, while high yield ('HY') credit remained a bright spot, supported by solid fundamental factors and low default risk.

Outlook

The global economy enters 2026 with moderate yet resilient growth. The United States remains the leader thanks to strong capital investment supported by full expensing, onshoring, and transformative spending on AI and automation. Productivity gains should help offset demographic pressures while consumption should stay solid. Inflation in the U.S. is gradually declining, aided by technological effects and lower housing costs, allowing the Fed to continue a gentle easing cycle. The labor market is showing signs of cooling but should remain stable, which supports a soft-landing scenario.

U.S. equities should benefit from solid growth, lower inflation, and fiscal as well as monetary impulses. Large companies remain the core of portfolios with exposure to AI, automation, and digital infrastructure. Valuations are high but look moderate when adjusted for record margins. We favor a balanced approach between growth and value. Smaller cap companies' earnings prospects are improving as financial conditions ease, but these segments face headwinds from higher input costs and less diversified revenue streams.

Globally, we maintain diversification. Europe faces structural challenges and slower growth although selected sectors, such as industrials and luxury goods, remain attractive. The United Kingdom faces a tougher environment with limited consumption and muted equity prospects. Japan stands out for stability, corporate governance reforms, and automation trends.

EM show mixed dynamics. China remains under pressure from real estate weakness and soft consumption yet cost advantages and export leadership support long-term potential. India continues as a key structural growth story while Latin America offers selective opportunities in commodities and nearshoring.

Fixed income fundamental factors for investment grade remain stable as issuers continue to show solid credit profiles, supported by steady revenue and earnings growth outside commodity sectors. While rating downgrades have recently outpaced upgrades, most large borrowers—particularly those investing heavily in AI—retain strong balance sheets and robust cash flow, limiting near-term credit stress. In HY, defaults remain near historical lows, and issuers generally exhibit healthy liquidity and balance sheet strength, though refinancing activity and tighter spreads warrant careful credit selection. The U.S. dollar is expected to weaken gradually due to Fed easing, overvaluation, and improving global growth.

Positioning

At the beginning of the period, Portfolios held modest tactical equity overweights and fixed income underweights relative to their strategic allocation benchmarks. Portfolios were also tactically overweight to U.S. large cap, international developed and U.S. long term Treasury bonds. Trusts were underweight to U.S. mid cap equities and core fixed income. During the period, international and EM equities were increased while U.S. equities and bonds were reduced to capture more attractive relative valuations abroad and mitigate concentration risk after a period of strong U.S. performance. European markets offer potential catalysts, including fiscal stimulus and easing energy pressures, while a softer dollar and a dovish Fed stance should support emerging market assets. These changes also increased exposure beyond U.S. large-cap AI themes and optimize EM positioning across vintages. Also, a modest position in an S&P 500 Equal Weight exposure was added and the position in traditional S&P 500 was reduced to mitigate concentration risk in mega-cap stocks and improve diversification. This adjustment increases exposure to broader market participation as breadth improves and positions the portfolio for potential mean reversion, should leadership expand beyond the largest constituents.

Overall, Portfolios hold a balanced posture between equity and fixed income, with overweights to U.S. large cap, U.S. mid cap, international developed equity and core fixed income, and underweights to real estate investment trusts ('REIT'), U.S. small cap and international bonds.

There were two manager changes during the period. We added Voya Inflation Protected Bond Plus to Voya Solution Income Portfolio after Voya's fixed income team assumed management in December 2024, implemented a strengthened process, and delivered solid results since the transition. With growing conviction in its ability to generate sustainable long-term outperformance, we are increasing our allocation as opportunities arise. We removed VY T. Rowe Price (TROW) Growth Equity as it sustained underperformance and a loss of investment continuity following the departure of long-tenured PM, with the strategy now led by a less experienced manager. Merging the T. Rowe Price Portfolio into the Voya Large Cap Growth Portfolio provides shareholders with a more experienced and historically stronger-performing team, along with benefits such as a lower net expense ratio, category continuity, and the potential for improved long-term results.

Performance

The Voya Solution Portfolios' primary performance objective is to outperform its strategic allocation benchmark over the long-term through tactical asset allocation, i.e., deviating from the composite benchmark over the short and medium-term and active manager selection. The benchmark return is the weighted average return of indices that represent asset classes included in the strategic allocation benchmark. Index returns are gross of all fees. The Portfolios are generally rebalanced monthly and the strategic asset allocations are updated annually to reflect changes to our capital market assumptions. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Portfolios outperformed their strategic allocation benchmarks on a gross- and net-of-fees basis. Tactical asset allocation and manager selection contributed.

Tactical asset allocation had a positive impact on performance during the period. Portfolios' tactical underweight to midcap and overweight to international developed equities were the main contributors. Mid-cap performance lagged largely because the segment had only modest participation in AI-related market leadership, faced softer earnings trends, and remained concentrated in sectors like industrials and consumer staples, which offered limited catalysts during the period. Cheaper starting valuations and improving economic conditions in international developed markets helped boost returns relative to the U.S. A tactical overweight to duration was the biggest detractor as the U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened with the long end rising modestly.

Underlying managers' relative results were positive across the Portfolios. Strategies that contributed most to excess returns in the quarter were Voya Multi-Manager Emerging Markets Equity, Vanguard FTSE Developed Market ETF (VEA) and Voya Large Cap Value. The biggest detractors in the quarter were Voya Multi-Manager International Equity, VY T. Rowe Price Diversified Mid Cap Growth and Vanguard FTSE Emerging Market ETF (VWO).

data.title: Expand Energy: Buy On The Post-Earnings Dip
data.description:
Expand Energy: Buy on Post-Earnings Dip

Expand Energy Corporation (EXE) just put up one of its strongest quarters, and somehow the stock is cheaper today than before earnings.

The company's net income came in at $553 million and operating cash flow at $956 million on production of 7.4 Bcfe/d. Full-year adjusted EBITDAX hit $5.1 billion. These are big numbers for a stock that the market seems to be discounting.

I see this as an excellent entry point for investors, however.

Shares have pulled back roughly 14% from their 52-week high of $126.62, dragged down by news of its CEO's departure on February 9 and a $0.75/Mcf slide in the gas strip since late November. I think the sell-off is overdone here.

The reasons for its dip—CEO departure and softer gas strip prices—are all temporary. None of them changes their strong operations or the natural gas demand story (which has only improved and will continue to improve), and this kind of dislocation is when long-term investors build positions.

On the Q4 earnings call, Interim CEO Mike Wichterich laid out a natural gas demand picture that should make every energy investor pay attention: “We are seeing 35% to 40% [demand growth] in the next five years,” with SVP Daniel Turco adding, “We are seeing about 25 billion cubic feet a day of gas demand coming online” from LNG, power, and industrial markets.

I'm starting coverage on Expand Energy with a BUY rating and a $150 price target, and I explain why below.

Expand Energy: What the Earnings Report Confirms

The Q4 earnings release was good by any measure.

Net production of 7.4 Bcfe/d was up 15% year-over-year, with its gas mix at 92%. Adjusted EPS of $2.00 beat the consensus of $1.88. Operating cash flow came in at $956 million, which is more than double what they did in Q4 2024.

For the full year, we're looking at $1.82 billion in net income, $5.08 billion in adjusted EBITDAX, and $4.58 billion in operating cash flow. On top of that, Expand returned $865 million to shareholders while still knocking $660 million off gross debt. That's a management team doing what they said they'd do.

And it's setting up to be a strong 2026, too. Expand is targeting output of 7.5 Bcfe/d off of capex of $2.85 billion.

The CEO, Wichterich, chimed in on operational execution on the company earnings call. “We have a 15% reduction in our breakevens in the Haynesville. That is very difficult to do. It doesn't just help our reinvestment rate. It also helps our inventory.”

COO Josh Viets added the company has “been able to add five years of inventory below $3.50” in a single year, expanding their economic drilling runway even at lower gas prices.

This next point might be the most underappreciated part of the story. Management formally outlined a $0.20/Mcf improvement target on realized gas prices, confirming roughly $500 million in incremental EBITDA.

Leadership Transition and Houston Relocation

On February 9, CEO Nick Dell’Osso stepped down related to the company's relocation to Houston, and Chairman Michael Wichterich took the Interim CEO role.

Dell’Osso deserves credit, and it's bittersweet to see him go. After all, he grew EXE from a $5 billion business to $26 billion.

The market didn’t like the news. Shares dropped nearly 6%, which I think was a knee-jerk reaction.

Wichterich addressed the elephant in the room head-on: “Call it six [months] is the goal [to find a replacement CEO]. I wouldn't be surprised if it went to nine months. I am committed to find the right person. I will be here until that occurs.”

The Houston relocation announcement is the other piece people are underweighting. Wichterich was blunt: “Moving to Houston is to be in that room, and so we have to get out of our own way.”

Houston is where the LNG deals happen and where the capital markets live. I don't see the relocation or CEO departure as negatives for the stock long-term; rather, as short-term blips.

AI and Data Center Demand

Here’s where the story gets really interesting for Expand Energy.

The IEA projects U.S. data center electricity demand will more than double from 183 TWh in 2024 to roughly 426 TWh by 2030, accounting for nearly half of all U.S. electricity demand growth.

Also, the DOE estimated 100 GW of new peak capacity is needed by 2030, with half from data centers, requiring 10–20% more U.S. gas production.

On the conference call, Turco connected this directly to Expand’s positioning: “Half of that [demand growth] is coming from LNG, and that sits right in our backyard...right where our Haynesville asset is.”

Viets added that “the inventory that we carry in the Haynesville is just simply unmatched...both in terms of depth and quality.” They’re in the right basin at the right time.

LNG Exports Add a Second Structural Demand Driver

The U.S. is building out a massive wave of new LNG export capacity. Total capacity is expected to jump from about 15.5 Bcf/d today to over 25 Bcf/d by 2028, with five projects already under construction adding 9.7 Bcf/d. That kind of buildout has real pricing implications: Henry Hub is forecast to roughly double from $2.20/MMBtu in 2024 to around $4.50 in 2026.

When asked on the call where all this demand is coming from, Wichterich didn't try to narrow it down: “LNG is definitely manufacturing. It is power. I think it is all. And so all of the above.”

For Expand Energy, with deep Haynesville inventory sitting right next to Gulf Coast LNG terminals, this is about as direct a beneficiary as you'll find in the gas space.

Focused on Improving the Balance Sheet

The balance sheet is in great shape: $613 million in cash, a $3.5 billion revolver extended to 2030, and gross debt down by $1.25 billion since the merger, with a target of at least $1 billion more coming off in 2026.

The $0.575/share quarterly dividend ($2.30 annualized) marks 20 consecutive quarters of payments.

Expand Energy: The Valuation

Expand Energy carries a forward P/E of 11.16 currently, according to YCharts, which is well below some of its natural gas peers listed above.

The stock also trades at an EV/EBITDA of just 4.75 (on a forward basis), according to Seeking Alpha. You could certainly make the case here that the stock is cheap.

I don’t see any reason why its shares should be trading at a discount to its peers. If anything, I think the stock needs to trade at a slight premium. This is North America’s largest gas producer, sitting at the center of a structural demand wave from AI and data centers.

Risks

The risks to owning Expand are the same as other natural gas stocks. Mainly, a substantial drop in natural gas prices below $3.00/Mcf would certainly hurt its cash flow and potentially lead to smaller dividends and less share buybacks (not to mention a lower stock price).

The CEO replacement overhang might continue to weigh on shares, too. The company needs to find a good replacement sooner rather than later.

There's also potential M&A risk. Wichterich noted in the conference call that the company “looked at a lot of transactions” in 2025 and “passed on a lot,” which I think shows discipline, but M&A execution risk is always present. Hopefully, the company will continue to be selective and only pursue opportunities with a clear ROI for Expand.

These are all real concerns, but I believe they're all manageable as well.

Future Catalysts to Watch

Pay attention to these key catalysts expected in 2026.

  • A permanent CEO appointment within six to nine months, removing the leadership overhang (I would guess they find a new CEO within 6–7 months).
  • Realization of the $0.20/Mcf gas marketing uplift, which would be worth roughly $500 million in incremental EBITDA, according to the company.
  • Continued LNG export capacity buildout, with five projects under construction adding 9.7 Bcf/d.
  • Accelerating AI and data center power demand from hyperscaler capital spending.
  • At least $1 billion in additional debt reduction in 2026, based on the company's earnings call comments.
  • Natural gas strip improvement as 25 Bcf/d of structural demand growth absorbs supply.
  • Potential increase in shareholder returns as debt targets are met.
  • Potential M&A.

Expand Energy: The Bottom Line

Expand Energy is North America’s largest natural gas producer. The company just delivered a strong 2025. It’s paying down debt aggressively, chasing a $500 million EBITDA uplift through smarter gas marketing, and relocating to Houston as a strategic, long-term move.

With a 7x trailing EV/EBITDA with a forward P/E near 10x, the market is giving you a discount here on a company sitting right in the path of a structural gas supercycle set to benefit from AI data center buildout.

Expand Energy, along with its neighbor to the North, Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF), is my top natural gas stock pick. I view both as premier, high-quality producers with relatively low risk compared to peers. Both may not seem deeply undervalued, but valuations compare favorably to peers, and I believe investors are truly buying into quality companies that command and deserve a valuation premium.

I view EXE stock as a BUY around $100 per share.

data.title: Hartford Municipal Opportunities ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US fixed-income markets delivered positive total returns in the fourth quarter of 2025 as policy uncertainty, fiscal developments, and divergent central bank actions shaped the investment landscape. The US began the quarter with its longest government shutdown on record, which delayed key economic data and forced the Federal Reserve (Fed) to make policy decisions with limited visibility. The shutdown ended in mid-November, but data gaps persisted, adding to market uncertainty. US agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), investment-grade corporate and high-yield corporate bonds produced positive total returns. MBS and high yield outperformed duration1 -equivalent US Treasuries, while investment-grade corporate bonds underperformed as spreads2 widened. The Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index returned 1.41% during the quarter. The ratio of 10-year AAA general obligations (GOs) to 10-year Treasuries decreased from 69.1% to 66.7%, remaining below the long-term historical average of 86% over the last 20 years.*

Performance Summary

  • The Hartford Municipal Opportunities ETF outperformed the Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index during the quarter.
  • Duration and yield-curve positioning added to relative returns. The portfolio maintained an underweight in the front and belly of the curve and an overweight in longer maturities, which supported performance as shorter-maturity AAA municipal yields sold off and long yields rallied.
  • Security selection in high-yield healthcare and investment-grade industrial-development bonds also contributed modestly to relative returns.
  • Our underweight in investment-grade GO bonds partially offset the positive relative returns.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Most states ended fiscal year 2025 with revenues above forecast. Although they have been declining recently, strong reserves keep us positive overall.
  • Many municipal sectors have the ability to raise revenues quickly to address inflationary pressures. The more labor-constrained sectors such as healthcare and state and local governments will be more challenged if inflation persists, but local governments still have exceptional cash reserves, and hospitals are in the best financial position they have been in over the past 5 years.
  • Expect municipals to behave defensively in a downturn given their high quality and strong balance sheets.
  • Tax-exempts are somewhat attractive at the corporate tax rate vs. corporate bonds, particularly relative to their recent history and at longer maturities.
  • Valuations are fair to rich across the curve, with the long end presenting the most value.
  • Following a record-breaking year of issuance in 2025, we expect this trend to continue and to be met by continued demand. A sharp move higher in rates is likely to lead to outflows and a more challenging market environment.

data.title: Commodity Price Watch: February 2026
data.description:
What is the overall trend in commodity prices?

Prices will be flat in 2026 for most globally traded industrial commodities. Crude oil will dip into mid-2026 on oversupply. Rising trade barriers will continue to be an important factor for buyers. The US led in 2025; now, the movement for 2026 is, to some extent, the rest of the world joining the party

As measured by the Materials Price Index (MPI) by S&P Global Market Intelligence, industrial materials prices bottomed in the second quarter of 2025 and will revisit those levels in the second quarter of this year.

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the MPI was 5% below its year-earlier level. Prices are returning to year-earlier levels, however, as the MPI for the second quarter of 2026 is forecast to be 0.4% below the previous year.

With pockets of oversupply in some areas and tight fundamentals in others, there is no overarching trend for industrial commodities. The most general summary is that 2026 spend will be quite similar to 2025, if the basket looks similar to the MPI, although country-specific tariffs will lift spend where the tariffs have gone up.

A relatively new and very important feature of commodity markets is extreme regionality. Historically, commodities were traded globally, and similarities outweighed differences between regions. Protectionism and logistics disruption have changed this global view. There is also variation across category fundamentals.

What is our forecast for energy-related commodity prices?

A global supply surplus averaging just under 1 million barrels per day for 2026 will continue to drive prices down over the year. In the shorter term, geopolitics is shifting prices. The first quarter of 2026 will see Brent crude prices averaging $63/barrel.

This is elevated relative to expectations because of tensions between the US and Iran leading to a risk premium. The most likely scenario is that the risk premium erodes as market fears of a supply disruption ease. This is far from certain, though, and some military intervention could send prices higher.

Venezuelan exports will grow modestly by the end of 2027 relative to the 2025 average, helping ease global prices marginally. India will reportedly stop importing Russian crude owing to a trade deal with the US, but Russian barrels will likely still find their way onto global markets.

What is our forecast for chemical prices?

The majority of pricing series in February experienced an upward revision in the near term compared with last month, largely driven by significantly higher spot crude oil pricing in recent weeks. The most notable example is with benzene and benzene derivatives, which makes sense given how closely benzene normally tracks crude oil.

Still, there are exceptions. The most pronounced one is North American ethylene pricing, which saw a significant downward revision because of upstream natural gas coming in softer than anticipated so far in 2026. In addition, some modest downward revisions were put in place for a few European markets because of an overabundance of supply.

What is our forecast for steel prices?

Steel will be oversupplied globally, but high import barriers in the US and the EU support elevated pricing. Prices rise in Asia, but slowly and from a low starting point. Supply is ample and idle capacity is rampant, so shortages are unlikely. Prices trend upward based on production restraint and rising protectionism. However, protectionism causes price weakness in net exporting markets.

Demand will remain subdued well into 2026. Expect demand for most end markets to be weak enough that it will not alone cause upward price pressure in the European Union, the United States, and mainland China.

There is stronger demand in India and Southeast Asia, but their expansion is insufficient to offset the wider weakness elsewhere. Mills are restraining output to limit surplus, leaving much capacity idle. Prices barely cover the cost of production in key regions such as East Asia, and mills often sell at a loss.

What is our forecast for nonferrous metal prices?

Aluminum prices remain elevated on a combination of tight supply and bullish financial speculation. Prices reached a four-year high at the end of January before retreating. We expect them to hold at high levels for the rest of the first quarter before starting to ease on improving supply, with speculators turning less positive on metals and taking profits.

These developments are becoming visible, with inventories ticking upward and financial positioning on exchanges turning more neutral. However, the return will be slow and anemic, rather than a sharp correction downward.

Copper prices remain elevated on a combination of tight supply, tariff uncertainty, a weaker US dollar and financial speculation. These drivers are largely unchanged from January’s report. However, the strength of upward price pressures has waned, with prices stepping down and cycling rather than pushing incessantly upward.

Risks remain elevated. Supply that is mostly balanced could turn negative on any supply reductions, becoming tied up in financing or tariff policies, which may change at short notice.

The Lunar New Year holiday should bring a lull in industrial demand and a temporary letup in speculatory activity, as mainland Chinese traders take a break, but adds to the uncertainty when they return.

With contributions from Keyla Goodno

data.title: Cloudflare: AI Traffic And Workers Driving Upside
data.description:
Cloudflare (NET) reported extremely strong results in the fourth quarter, leading the stock to rebound modestly from its recent drawdown. Current strength appears to be broad-based, with application services an area of particular strength. This is before tailwinds like AI inferencing workloads begin to have a material impact.

Concerns over AI disrupting software companies could weigh on the stock in the near term. This fear is misplaced in Cloudflare's case, though, due to the infrastructure-heavy nature of its business and the competitive advantages it has.

I previously suggested that the Cloudflare bubble had reinflated. While the stock is now up since then, there has been significant volatility and opportunities to enter at lower prices. I continue to believe that investors need to be prepared for a substantial drawdown, but emerging tailwinds likely mean that Cloudflare will provide strong returns going forward.

Market Conditions

Despite growing concerns about the potential impact of AI on software, the demand environment appears to be improving for most software companies, with AI and cybersecurity remaining areas of particular strength.

Cloudflare has stated a number of times in the past that it plans on investing through its income statement, holding its operating income near breakeven for the foreseeable future. As a result, the company's hiring should be a reasonable indicator of revenue growth. The number of Cloudflare job openings steadily increased over the past 12 months and potentially points towards stronger revenue growth going forward.

This is supported by the number of job openings mentioning Cloudflare in the job requirements. After a sharp pullback in late 2022, this metric has been steadily increasing in recent years.

While Cloudflare's business is accelerating and the demand environment is supportive, its share price has been collateral damage of the uncertainty that AI has created for software investors.

I believe that this fear is misplaced in Cloudflare's case, though, as it is more of an infrastructure company, operating an increasingly complex network. Cloudflare handles roughly 20% of all internet traffic and invested 14% of revenue in its network in the third quarter. The company's network currently has 449 Tbps capacity and a presence in over 330 cities.

While it may take time for the market to discriminate, it is application companies that are most at risk, particularly if they only offer a simple interface on top of a system of record. In addition, much of Cloudflare's business stands to benefit from increased utilization rather than being pressured by seat-based pricing.

Cloudflare Business Updates

Cloudflare's dominant position in handling internet traffic means that it is well positioned to capitalize on the growing importance of crawlers and bot management. The company is adapting its platform to protect and manage content in the AI age, which includes introducing a Pay Per Crawl revenue generator for content creators. In support of this, Cloudflare is acquiring Human Native for its data marketplace.

Cloudflare also plans on implementing solutions for agentic commerce. Payments between agents may benefit from a specifically designed currency that is instant, global, secure, and facilitates microtransactions. Cloudflare has launched the NET Dollar project, although it is likely to work with partners for regulatory reasons. NET Dollar is a programmable USD-backed stablecoin that is designed for agentic commerce and will also enable creators to be rewarded for original content. While I am not particularly bullish on this initiative, Cloudflare is positioned to drive adoption through developers.

The company also has partnerships with Coinbase (COIN), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), and American Express (AXP) based around agent-to-agent payments. In addition, Cloudflare is contributing to open standards such as the Agent Payments Protocol (Google) and x402 to simplify the process of sending and receiving payments on the Internet.

Cloudflare's Workers platform continues to expand at a rapid pace, with AI inference a growth driver. The platform is also being used to build AI agents and full-stack applications. Cloudflare had over 4.5 million developers on its platform at the end of 2025 and stands to benefit if vibe coding leads to an increase in applications. Cloudflare plans on acquiring Replicate, an AI platform for developers, which will provide users of the Workers platform access to AI models.

Cloudflare's transition from product-led growth to enterprise sales continues, removing one of the few real sources of doubt about the company. This is demonstrated by the record number of customers spending in excess of 1 million USD annually that have been added in recent quarters. The company also closed its largest deal ever in terms of annual contract value (42.5 million USD per year) in Q4. Cloudflare's sales capacity is growing at the fastest rate since 2022, but despite this, Cloudflare's sales team achieved its highest quota attainment in the last 4 years in Q4.

The sales force transformation is most relevant to Cloudflare's SASE solution, which is now feature complete. It isn't really clear how this product is performing, but Cloudflare has suggested that there have been no significant changes in the competitive landscape.

Financial Analysis

Cloudflare generated 614.5 million USD revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 34% YoY, and new ACV increased nearly 50% YoY, the highest rate since 2021. While AI inferencing is growing rapidly, it is still a small portion of Cloudflare's business at the moment.

Large customers are an area of particular strength, with revenue from large customers (>100,000 USD annual spend) increasing 42% YoY in Q4. This is notable as Cloudflare's ability to transition to drive enterprise sales has been a question mark. A growing percentage of revenue is also coming through channel partners, although this is still a relatively immature part of the business relative to peers like Zscaler (ZS).

Cloudflare expects 620-621 million USD revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 29-30% YoY. For the full year, the company expects 2.785-2.795 billion USD revenue, implying 28-29% growth. Cloudflare is also targeting 5 billion USD annualized revenue by the end of 2028 (a little over 25% annual revenue growth). Guidance is likely to prove overly conservative, though, and I expect Cloudflare's growth to approach 40% in the second half of the year.

Expansion of Cloudflare's customer base has also picked up in recent quarters, with the company's largest customer cohorts an area of particular strength. Cloudflare also continues to drive healthy expansion within existing customers, supported by a product portfolio that now includes more than 55 revenue-generating products. Cloudflare's dollar-based net retention rate was 120% in Q4, up 1% sequentially.

Cloudflare's gross margin has ticked lower in recent quarters, which could be indicative of new product growth. In addition, paid traffic has increased relative to free user traffic, meaning more network expense is being allocated to COGS. While this makes sense from an accounting perspective, it reduces the interpretability of gross margin changes.

Cloudflare's non-GAAP operating profit was almost 90 million USD in the fourth quarter at a 14.6% margin. The company also generated almost 100 million USD free cash flow in the quarter. While Cloudflare's margins are generally improving, the company continues to balance profitability and growth, which could mean that increased investments limit further gains in the short term. The company's 4.1 billion USD cash position and positive cash flows position it to do this. Cloudflare has suggested that it is targeting 20%-plus operating margins and 25%-plus free cash flow margins long-term.

Conclusion

Cloudflare still has a high valuation, particularly in comparison to a number of its peers that have faced significant multiple compression in recent years. The breadth of Cloudflare's business and its robust financial performance make comparisons difficult, though. Cloudflare is seeing stronger AI tailwinds than network security peers and is better positioned to capitalize on the edge compute opportunity than CDN peers.

Cloudflare's competitive advantages have also become better appreciated by the market in recent years, which likely ensures the company maintains its valuation premium. There is significant downside risk if investor sentiment further sours, though, and there is no guarantee that the company won't continue to be dragged down by AI fears. Despite this, I believe that Cloudflare will offer solid double-digit returns for investors willing to hold through any near-term volatility.

data.title: BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
"As markets may face volatility with geopolitical, monetary, and tariff policy outcomes unfolding, we remain confident in the long-term growth trajectories and fundamental business strength across our portfolio."

Market Review

The MSCI World Index 3.12% in a volatile quarter, as better-than-expected corporate earnings propelled global equities despite several growing risks. The quarter's strong earnings season painted a picture of a resilient global economy, but concerns of a potential artificial intelligence (AI) bubble and a more cautious consumer dampened the outlook. Central bank policy continues to be accommodative while trade tensions cooled in the quarter, but both themes face uncertainty in the year ahead.

Volatility was heightened in the quarter with investors scrutinizing key performance themes, including AI investments and consumer resiliency. Many large technology companies reported strong revenue and earnings growth during the period and pledged to increase capital expenditures, as tremendous computing demand outstrips current supply. Beneficiaries of the AI boom broadened out across the U.S., Europe, and Asia to include semiconductor equipment, memory, and component providers who reported strong earnings. As a corollary to earnings results, a series of partnerships between OpenAI and public technology companies were announced throughout the quarter that totaled over $1 trillion in future spending on AI chips, datacenters, and cloud computing. Investor concerns arose around the circular nature of funding deals, increased debt burdens, and the uncertain return profile of these investments. Notably, strong earnings from bellwether NVIDIA resulted in a muted market response, suggesting that investors became more discerning of AI-related investments this quarter.

Outside of information technology, the industrials sector also benefited from continued data center construction and investments made to modernize the electric grid. Within the financials sector, major banks saw earnings growth driven by strong loan and advisory activity, while some information services providers came under pressure from perceived AI threats to their business. Retail-facing companies reported higher levels of consumer uncertainty as high interest rates delayed large purchase decisions, such as home renovation projects. Consumer reports highlighted an increasingly pronounced bifurcation, with higher-income consumers continuing to spend broadly while lower-income consumers sought out value and traded down. Companies have largely been able to absorb tariff costs to-date, but management teams warned that they will have to raise prices eventually to make up for increased expenses.

European earnings held up well and demonstrated the continent's ability to react and adapt to U.S. tariffs. The European Central Bank (ECB) and the Bank of England (BoE) began to cut rates early in the year, governments stepped in with large spending bills, and European companies trimmed costs and improved efficiency. In China, slower-than-expected economic growth continues under the strain of tariffs and an ailing real estate sector. However, China demonstrated its ability to successfully reroute goods to other markets as its trade surplus surpassed $1 trillion for the first time.

On trade policy, President Trump and Chinese Leader Xi met for their long-awaited meeting in South Korea and agreed to a truce that would pause tariffs for another year. In a further thawing of relations, the U.S. government approved the sale of scaled-down AI chips to China. President Trump also met Japan's new Prime Minister, Sanae Takaichi, whose political agenda is expected to expand fiscal policy, support wage growth to offset inflation, and focus on geopolitical security as it relates to military and AI spending. Japanese equities rallied on the prospect of these pro-growth policy positions. While trade tensions cooled, the oscillating nature of tariff negotiations and a looming U.S. Supreme Court decision remain a risk.

Many of the global central banks arrived at the conclusion of their current easing cycle during the fourth quarter. The ECB held rates steady and the BoE implemented a single 25 bp cut in the quarter. Both agencies signaled they are at the end of their easing cycles, which commenced at the start of the year to deal with U.S.-imposed tariffs. In the U.S., inflation remained above 2%; however, the low-hiring job market and unemployment rate increase to 4.6% prompted the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) to cut the Federal Funds rate by 25 bps both in October and December. The Fed believes the current Federal Funds rate range of 3.50-3.75% is relatively neutral and neither supportive nor a hindrance to growth, signaling a potential pause before further easing is warranted. The loosening of financial conditions successfully buttressed the world's major economies against the impact of U.S. tariffs, but investors may not be able to rely on policy support going forward.

Investors paid greater attention to the multitude of risks this quarter, including a potential AI bubble, deteriorating employment data, and prolonged uncertainty over tariffs. Despite these challenges, strong corporate earnings drove global equity gains

Performance Review

The Fund trailed the Index in the period, driven by an overall negative stock selection effect. The Fund benefited from a positive stock selection effect within the consumer discretionary and communication services sectors, resulting in positive relative results. Within the real estate sector, a favorable underweight allocation supported relative performance.

Conversely, the Fund's disadvantageous stock selection across the industrials, information technology, and financials sectors detracted from relative performance.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance include Alphabet (GOOGL), LVMH (LVMHF), Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Apple (AAPL).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Microsoft (MSFT), BAE Systems (BAESF)(BAESY), Nintendo (NTDOF)(NTDOY), Meta Platforms (META), and Eaton Corp. (ETN).

Market Outlook

The Fund's investment philosophy remains consistent during periods of political and economic transformation. Governments across the world are rewriting economic and foreign policies and transforming trade relationships that have anchored stability in the preceding decades. These changes have resulted in unprecedented uncertainty and volatility. Harking back on our decades of investment experience, especially during uncertain or volatile markets, we remain confident in the industry-leading businesses that comprise our portfolio. We believe long-term value is driven by companies that can consistently grow earnings through various business cycles. We will continue to stay vigilant and invest in accordance with our investment philosophy, as we have done during previous periods of transformation.

data.title: Genpact: ATS Growth And Margin Expansion Strengthen The Investment Case
data.description:
Genpact Limited (G) is continuing to execute well against its ambition, and the results in the last fiscal year are proof that my confidence in the business is not misplaced. To be fair, the narrative shift has taken some patience to fully support, and I don’t think the market is fully onboard yet.

However, I’d say that the waiting phase is over, and the business now looks measurably different from a few years ago. As such, I’m reiterating my Buy stance on the stock.

FY2025 Results: Pivot Now in Numbers

Full-Year FY2025 Snapshot

First, revenue isn’t growing at a breakneck pace, but we must remember that this is a company that is shifting towards higher-value technology and AI-led services, so the topline will take some time to reflect that. For me, the mix shift and margin expansion are more important, and they’re not accidental. I’ll discuss them in a bit more detail shortly, but management explicitly tied the margin expansion to a higher-value ATS mix and disciplined cost execution during the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

Q3 2025 Revenue Breakdown

Q4 2025 Snapshot
